rationale: The citing paper explicitly discusses Khan and Dhar (2006) to support its claim that behavior carryover effects can operate nonconsciously, stating that consumers may be unaware of how prior conduct shapes later choices. It then cites Khan and Dhar again when justifying its own awareness-testing approach (open-ended probing about factors influencing behavior) as an established method. Although the 2017 paper develops a different focal phenomenon (upgrade-induced carelessness), this citation plays a meaningful conceptual and methodological role rather than a purely perfunctory mention.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Khan and Dhar (2006) as prior evidence that sequential consumer-choice effects can be nonconscious, and leverages that precedent to motivate and structure its tests of whether upgrade-driven carelessness occurs without explicit awareness.
